GenAI Cybersecurity Review - DRAFT version

Intro

This is meant as an inventory/tracking on Cybersecurity-related information on the several aspects of GenAI.

This page should evolve over time. It is NOT meant to be either comprehensive nor perfect (or even orderly) in any way or fashion, but instead helps me keep a bit of tracking.

Some random thoughts here:

  • LLM-based “autonomy” is generally a bad idea. Even in perfect settings, the LLM can misbehave and skip its mandate(s), it has happened. Or hallucinate. Now consider any possibility for prompt injection (we’re talking single-channel here, remember) and you see where things can go wrong…

  • Guardrails on Free-text are for obvious reason imperfect. i.e. Red-Teaming an LLM for injection seems unnecessary: Prompt injection is inevitable?

  • For privacy & security controls, somehow put a Man-In-The-Middle, deterministic (NER, etc.) control to anonymize data you send to an LLM inference step.

  • Be granular in what the MCP, A2A, skills, what-not can and cannot do.

  • Do not give an “agent” (concept that could be discussed in itself) any permission it doesn’t absolutely NEED to do its job. If you don’t want an Agent to “rm -rf”, don’t rely on trusting the LLM. Give it a RBAC that ensures it simply CAN’T do that.

  • Re-authenticate often.

  • Inventory MCP. Audit stuff. It won’t protect you, but it will help Incident Response.

  • Frankly: Cybersecurity is one-step-behind. Particularly in “Agentic” stuff. Even if theory is being worked on, vendors sell products, etc.

  • …

From Vendors

E.g. MCP

E.g. Cowork

As stated here (as of end of Feb 2026) https://claude.com/blog/cowork-research-preview

“That said, there are still things to be aware of before you give Claude control. By default, the main thing to know is that Claude can take potentially destructive actions (such as deleting local files) if it’s instructed to. Since there’s always some chance that Claude might misinterpret your instructions, you should give Claude very clear guidance around things like this. 

You should also be aware of the risk of “prompt injections”: attempts by attackers to alter Claude’s plans through content it might encounter on the internet. We’ve built sophisticated defenses against prompt injections, but agent safety—that is, the task of securing Claude’s real-world actions—is still an active area of development in the industry. 

These risks aren’t new with Cowork, but it might be the first time you’re using a more advanced tool that moves beyond a simple conversation. We recommend taking precautions, particularly while you learn how it works. We provide more detail in our Help Center.”

E.g. Cursor

https://cursor.com/blog/agent-sandboxing

E.g. OpenClaw

Dixit the vendor itself: There is no “perfectly secure” setup.
